Sandbox Environments ŌĆö Containing the Blast Radius

Module 2.3: Sandbox Environments ŌĆö Containing the Blast Radius

Section titled ŌĆ£Module 2.3: Sandbox Environments ŌĆö Containing the Blast RadiusŌĆØ

Estimated time: ~40 minutes

Prerequisite: Module 2.2 (Permission System)

Outcome: After this module, you will be able to run Claude Code in an isolated Docker environment that limits the blast radius of any mistake or malicious action

1. WHY ŌĆö Why This Matters

Section titled ŌĆ£1. WHY ŌĆö Why This MattersŌĆØ

You now understand the blast radius (Module 2.1) and permission prompts (Module 2.2). But permissions are reactive ŌĆö you must catch every dangerous request and say ŌĆ£no.ŌĆØ That requires perfect discipline every single time. One moment of distraction, one misleading prompt from Claude, one permission you approve thinking itŌĆÖs safe ŌĆö and the damage is done.

Sandboxing is proactive defense. Instead of relying on you to block bad actions, sandboxing makes dangerous actions impossible by design. Permissions are your seatbelt. Sandbox is the airbag. Even if you make a mistake ŌĆö even if Claude tricks you into approving rm -rf ~/.ssh ŌĆö the sandbox contains the blast radius. The host system stays safe.

If you work with client code, handle sensitive data, or simply value your machineŌĆÖs integrity, sandboxing is not optional. ItŌĆÖs the difference between ŌĆ£I hope I catch every mistakeŌĆØ and ŌĆ£mistakes canŌĆÖt escape this box.ŌĆØ

2. CONCEPT ŌĆö Core Ideas

Section titled ŌĆ£2. CONCEPT ŌĆö Core IdeasŌĆØ

Why Sandbox Over Permissions Alone

Section titled ŌĆ£Why Sandbox Over Permissions AloneŌĆØ
ApproachModelFailure Mode
Permissions onlyReactive ŌĆö you must catch every bad requestOne approval mistake = full compromise
SandboxProactive ŌĆö bad requests canŌĆÖt reach outside the boxDamage contained to sandbox environment
Both (defense in depth)Layered securityMistake must bypass multiple barriers

Permissions require human vigilance. Sandboxes enforce isolation automatically. Best practice: use both.

How Sandboxing Works

Section titled ŌĆ£How Sandboxing WorksŌĆØ

A sandbox is an isolated environment where Claude Code runs. It can only access what you explicitly give it. Everything else ŌĆö your home directory, SSH keys, AWS credentials, other projects ŌĆö doesnŌĆÖt exist from ClaudeŌĆÖs perspective.

Docker as Primary Sandbox

Section titled ŌĆ£Docker as Primary SandboxŌĆØ

Docker containers provide excellent isolation for development work:

  • Mount ONLY the project directory ŌĆö Claude sees only your project code
  • No network by default ŌĆö prevents data exfiltration (--network=none)
  • Resource limits ŌĆö prevents resource exhaustion attacks
  • Disposable ŌĆö destroy container after session (--rm)
  • No persistence ŌĆö secrets in container context disappear on exit

Critical rule: Never mount your home directory, ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, or any parent directory containing secrets.

Devcontainers (VS Code Integration)

Section titled ŌĆ£Devcontainers (VS Code Integration)ŌĆØ

Devcontainers (.devcontainer/devcontainer.json) provide team-sharable sandbox configurations:

  • Same isolation as Docker, but integrated with VS Code
  • Team members get identical, secure environments
  • Configuration lives in version control
  • Still requires careful mount configuration

Cloud Sandboxes

Section titled ŌĆ£Cloud SandboxesŌĆØ

ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Needs verification ŌĆö check current GitHub Codespaces / Gitpod support for Claude Code.

Cloud sandboxes (GitHub Codespaces, Gitpod) offer zero-setup isolation:

  • Your machine is never exposed ŌĆö environment is remote
  • Credentials managed by cloud platform
  • Disposable by design
  • Tradeoff: cost and network dependency

Sandbox Levels

Section titled ŌĆ£Sandbox LevelsŌĆØ
LevelDescriptionWhat Claude Can AccessRecommended For
0No sandboxEverything on your systemŌØī Never recommended
1Discipline onlyEverything (relies on you saying ŌĆ£noŌĆØ)ŌØī Quick tasks only, high risk
2Docker + project mountProject files onlyŌ£ģ Daily development
3Docker + no network + project mountProject files, no internetŌ£ģ Sensitive projects
4Cloud sandboxNothing on your machineŌ£ģ Client work, untrusted code

3. DEMO ŌĆö Step by Step

Section titled ŌĆ£3. DEMO ŌĆö Step by StepŌĆØ

LetŌĆÖs build a Docker sandbox for Claude Code from scratch. This sandbox will isolate Claude to a single project directory with no network access.

Step 1: Create Dockerfile

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 1: Create DockerfileŌĆØ

Create a file named Dockerfile in an empty directory:

Why this matters: We use a minimal base image (node:20-slim), create a non-root user, and set /workspace as the working directory. This is where weŌĆÖll mount the project.

Expected output when building:

Step 2: Build the Image

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 2: Build the ImageŌĆØ

Why this matters: The -t flag tags the image with a name you can reference later. Building takes 2-5 minutes on first run.

Expected output:

Step 3: Run with Correct Mounts (CRITICAL)

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 3: Run with Correct Mounts (CRITICAL)ŌĆØ

This is the most security-critical step. The -v flag controls what Claude can access.

Breaking down each flag:

  • -it ŌĆö interactive terminal
  • --rm ŌĆö CRITICAL ŌĆö destroy container on exit (no secret persistence)
  • -v "$(pwd)":/workspace ŌĆö mount current directory ONLY, not home or parent
  • --network=none ŌĆö CRITICAL ŌĆö no internet = no data exfiltration
  • --memory=4g ŌĆö limit memory usage
  • --cpus=2 ŌĆö limit CPU usage

Expected output:

YouŌĆÖre now inside the container. Your prompt shows youŌĆÖre the developer user in /workspace.

Step 4: Verify Isolation ŌĆö Try to Access Host Secrets

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 4: Verify Isolation ŌĆö Try to Access Host SecretsŌĆØ

Inside the container, attempt to access common secret locations:

Expected output:

Expected output:

Expected output:

Why this matters: No .aws, no .ssh, no secrets. The container home directory is empty except for default shell configs. Host secrets are invisible.

Step 5: Verify Network Isolation

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 5: Verify Network IsolationŌĆØ

Attempt to reach the internet:

Expected output:

Why this matters: Even if Claude manages to execute a malicious command that tries to exfiltrate data via HTTP, it fails. No network = no data leaves the container.

Step 6: Verify Project Access

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 6: Verify Project AccessŌĆØ

Your project files should be visible:

Expected output:

Why this matters: Claude can read and modify project files (as intended), but nothing else. Blast radius is contained to this project only.

Step 7: Exit and Verify Cleanup

Section titled ŌĆ£Step 7: Exit and Verify CleanupŌĆØ

The container is destroyed (because of --rm). Any secrets that entered ClaudeŌĆÖs context during the session are gone. Start fresh next time with a clean environment.

4. PRACTICE ŌĆö Try It Yourself

Section titled ŌĆ£4. PRACTICE ŌĆö Try It YourselfŌĆØ

Exercise 1: Build Your First Sandbox

Section titled ŌĆ£Exercise 1: Build Your First SandboxŌĆØ

Goal: Create a working Docker sandbox for Claude Code and verify it runs.

Instructions:

  1. Create a new directory: mkdir ~/claude-sandbox-test && cd ~/claude-sandbox-test
  2. Create the Dockerfile from Step 1 of the DEMO section
  3. Build the image: docker build -t my-claude-sandbox .
  4. Run the container: docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace my-claude-sandbox
  5. Inside the container, run: pwd and ls -la ~

Expected result: You should see /workspace as your working directory, and the home directory should contain only default shell config files (no .aws, .ssh, etc.).

­¤ÆĪ Hint

If the build fails, check:

  • Is Docker installed and running? (docker --version)
  • Are you in the directory with the Dockerfile?
  • Does the Dockerfile have correct syntax (no tabs, proper indentation)?
Ō£ģ Solution

Success looks like this:

  • Build completes without errors
  • Container starts and drops you into a bash prompt
  • pwd shows /workspace
  • No secrets visible in home directory

Exercise 2: Test the Blast Radius

Section titled ŌĆ£Exercise 2: Test the Blast RadiusŌĆØ

Goal: Prove that secrets on your host are invisible to the container.

Instructions:

  1. On your HOST machine, create a fake secret: echo "SECRET_KEY=fake-api-key-12345" > ~/.fake-secret
  2. Start your sandbox container: docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace my-claude-sandbox
  3. Inside the container, try to read the secret: cat ~/.fake-secret
  4. Exit the container
  5. Verify the secret still exists on host: cat ~/.fake-secret

Expected result: Step 3 should fail with ŌĆ£No such file or directoryŌĆØ. Step 5 should succeed and show the secret. This proves the container cannot access host files outside the mount.

­¤ÆĪ Hint

The containerŌĆÖs home directory (~ inside container) is /home/developer, which is separate from your host home directory. Files in your hostŌĆÖs ~ are NOT visible unless you mount them with -v.

Ō£ģ Solution

What this proves: The containerŌĆÖs ~/.fake-secret is a different file than the hostŌĆÖs ~/.fake-secret. Container home (/home/developer) is isolated from host home. Secrets are safe.

Exercise 3: Verify Network Isolation

Section titled ŌĆ£Exercise 3: Verify Network IsolationŌĆØ

Goal: Confirm that --network=none actually prevents network access.

Instructions:

  1. Run container WITH network: docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace my-claude-sandbox
  2. Inside container, test network: curl -I https://google.com
  3. Exit container
  4. Run container WITHOUT network: docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace --network=none my-claude-sandbox
  5. Inside container, test network again: curl -I https://google.com

Expected result: Step 2 should succeed (youŌĆÖll see HTTP headers). Step 5 should fail with ŌĆ£Could not resolve hostŌĆØ. This proves --network=none works.

­¤ÆĪ Hint

The -I flag makes curl fetch only HTTP headers (faster test). If curl isnŌĆÖt installed in your container, install it first: apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl (requires running container as root or rebuilding Dockerfile).

Ō£ģ Solution

What this proves: --network=none actually blocks network access. Even if Claude Code (or malicious code) tries to exfiltrate data via HTTP, it fails silently.

5. CHEAT SHEET

Section titled ŌĆ£5. CHEAT SHEETŌĆØ

Essential Docker Sandbox Commands

Section titled ŌĆ£Essential Docker Sandbox CommandsŌĆØ
CommandPurposeSecurity Note
docker build -t name .Build sandbox imageOne-time setup
docker run -it --rmRun interactive, auto-cleanup--rm prevents secret persistence
-v "$(pwd)":/workspaceMount current directoryŌÜĀ’ĖÅ ONLY mount project, never ~
--network=noneDisable networkingPrevents data exfiltration
--memory=4gLimit RAM to 4GBPrevents resource exhaustion
--cpus=2Limit to 2 CPU coresPrevents resource exhaustion
-u $(id -u):$(id -g)Match host user UID/GIDFixes file permission issues

What to Mount vs. What to NEVER Mount

Section titled ŌĆ£What to Mount vs. What to NEVER MountŌĆØ
PathMount?Reason
$(pwd) (current project)Ō£ģ YESThis is your working directory
~/project-name (specific project)Ō£ģ YESExplicit, isolated project
~ (home directory)ŌØī NEVERContains all your secrets
~/.sshŌØī NEVERSSH keys exposed
~/.awsŌØī NEVERAWS credentials exposed
~/.configŌØī NEVERContains API keys, tokens
/var/run/docker.sockŌØī NEVERDocker socket = root on host
Parent directory with secretsŌØī NEVERSiblings might have .env files

Quick Sandbox Levels Reference

Section titled ŌĆ£Quick Sandbox Levels ReferenceŌĆØ
Use CaseCommand
Daily dev (Level 2)docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace sandbox
Sensitive project (Level 3)docker run -it --rm -v "$(pwd)":/workspace --network=none sandbox
Client work (Level 4)Use GitHub Codespaces or Gitpod (zero host exposure)

6. PITFALLS ŌĆö Common Mistakes

Section titled ŌĆ£6. PITFALLS ŌĆö Common MistakesŌĆØ
ŌØī MistakeŌ£ģ Correct Approach
Mounting home directory: -v ~:/homeMount ONLY project: -v "$(pwd)":/workspace ŌĆö home contains all secrets
Forgetting --rm flagAlways use --rm ŌĆö prevents containers with secrets from persisting on disk
Using --privileged flagNEVER use --privileged ŌĆö gives container root on host, defeats all isolation
Mounting Docker socket: -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sockNEVER mount Docker socket ŌĆö equivalent to root access on host
Forgetting --network=none for sensitive workAlways use --network=none for client work or sensitive data ŌĆö prevents exfiltration
Mounting parent directory: -v ~/projects:/workspaceMount specific project only: -v ~/projects/client-a:/workspace ŌĆö siblings might have secrets
Running as root user in containerCreate non-root user in Dockerfile (see DEMO) ŌĆö limits damage from container escape
Hardcoding secrets in DockerfileNEVER put secrets in Dockerfile ŌĆö they persist in image layers forever
Assuming --rm deletes image layers--rm deletes container, not image ŌĆö rebuild image if secrets leaked during build

7. REAL CASE ŌĆö Production Story

Section titled ŌĆ£7. REAL CASE ŌĆö Production StoryŌĆØ

Scenario: TechViet Solutions, a software outsourcing company in Ho Chi Minh City, works on multiple client projects simultaneously. Their team structure has developers working on 2-3 client projects per week. Each client has separate repositories, separate .env files, and strict NDA requirements.

Problem: Developer Susan is working on Client AŌĆÖs banking app codebase (~/projects/client-a/). She asks Claude Code: ŌĆ£Find all API endpoints that arenŌĆÖt using authentication middleware.ŌĆØ Claude Code, being thorough, searches broadly. It reads not just Client AŌĆÖs code, but accidentally traverses up to ~/projects/ and reads Client BŌĆÖs .env file in the sibling directory (~/projects/client-b/.env).

Client BŌĆÖs .env contains their payment gateway API key. This key is now in Claude CodeŌĆÖs context. It may be:

  • Logged to AnthropicŌĆÖs servers (for debugging)
  • Included in ClaudeŌĆÖs response if it thinks itŌĆÖs relevant
  • Cached in terminal scrollback
  • Visible in screenshots Susan takes for documentation

This is an NDA violation. TechViet must report to Client B that their credentials were exposed to an AI system and third-party servers. Client B demands a full security audit and threatens contract termination.

How Sandbox Would Have Prevented This:

If Susan had used Docker sandbox:

From inside this container:

  • Working directory is /workspace (which is Client AŌĆÖs code only)
  • ~/projects/client-b/ does not exist in the container
  • Claude CodeŌĆÖs search commands (find, grep, cat) can only see Client AŌĆÖs files
  • Even if Claude tries cat ~/projects/client-b/.env, it fails: ŌĆ£No such file or directoryŌĆØ

The blast radius is contained to Client A only. Cross-contamination is impossible by design, not by discipline.

Solution Implemented:

TechViet Solutions mandated Docker sandboxes for all client work:

  1. Each client project gets a pre-built sandbox image (Dockerfile in repo)
  2. Developers must use ./scripts/sandbox.sh script that enforces correct mounts
  3. CI/CD checks verify no .env files are committed
  4. Weekly audit: docker ps -a to check for containers without --rm flag

Result: No cross-client contamination incidents in 6 months. Developers initially complained about ŌĆ£extra steps,ŌĆØ but after one audit where a competitor was breached due to AI-assisted coding exposure, the team embraced it. Client confidence increased, and TechViet now markets ŌĆ£AI-safe development practicesŌĆØ as a competitive advantage.

Key lesson: Sandboxing isnŌĆÖt paranoia. ItŌĆÖs professional practice when handling client data. The 30 seconds to start a container is worth avoiding the career-ending mistake of leaking client secrets.

Next: Module 2.4: Secret Management ŌåÆ

Permission System Deep Dive Next
Secret Management ŌĆö Keeping Your Keys Away from Claude Code
